behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is whether the LLM actually generates code with the correct signature. Mocking the LLM to return 'correct' signatures would hide real-world cases where the LLM deviates from the spec. Only a real LLM call reveals whether the model faithfully translates natural language specs into matching signatures. Signature validation logic (Unit/Integration) is secondary; the core risk is LLM generation.
behaviors.test_description: Call the LLM with a function spec, capture the generated code, parse it with AST/introspection, and verify parameter names, types, and return type match the spec exactly.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is whether the LLM generates code that actually passes the test suite. Mocking the LLM to return 'working' code and mocking the test runner to pass would hide real cases where generated code fails tests. The behavior depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. Executing tests against hardcoded code (Integration) doesn't test the generation side. A single real LLM call is sufficient to verify this behavior (even if tests pass on first try).
behaviors.test_description: Call the LLM to generate a function implementation, run it against provided test cases, and verify the final code (after zero or more attempts) passes all tests.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: This tests the wiring between test failure output and prompt construction, not LLM quality. The real failure is in the code that pipes test results into the next prompt (e.g., 'Test failed: AssertionError on line X'). You can mock both LLM calls and provide a real test runner (or mock test runner with failure output), then verify the prompt structure includes the failures. The risk is in prompt construction logic and data flow, not in LLM behavior, so real LLM calls aren't necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the first LLM call to return failing code, mock the test runner to return specific failure messages, capture the prompt sent to the second LLM call, and verify it includes those failure messages in readable form.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: The real failures are in control flow: Does the retry counter actually stop at 3? Is the error structure complete? These are logic and state-machine questions, not LLM-dependent. Mock both the LLM and test runner (with failures), run through 3 attempts, and verify that the service stops and returns the right error shape. This can be done with in-process mocks. Unit tests could verify counter logic in isolation, but Integration-level testing of the full wired flow is more robust.
behaviors.test_description: Mock LLM to return failing code and mock test runner to fail all attempts, trigger the service 3 times, verify it stops after the 3rd attempt and returns a structured error containing all 3 attempts with their test results.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: This is the signature test of the system: Does iterative refinement actually work? A single Agentic call (first generation) would fail for the hard function by design. The real failure is that multiple feedback loops don't improve the output, or that refinement is broken. You must run a sequence of real LLM calls (generate → test failure → refine → test again), each conditioned on previous outputs. Mocked or hardcoded code cannot test whether feedback-driven improvement works. This is multiple agentic steps in sequence — Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Select or define a function that a single LLM call typically fails to implement correctly. Run the full loop with real LLM calls: generate code, run tests, if failed provide feedback and refine, test again, repeat. Verify that after 1–3 iterations, the code passes all tests, demonstrating feedback-driven improvement.
